content.full_text:
DARRYL R. WOLD
ATTORNEY AT LAW
318 AIRWAY AVENUE, SUITE M-I
COSTA MESA, CALIFORNIA 92626
(714) 641-4942    FAX (714) 546-1003


May 17, 1990


Jill Stecher, Esq.
Fair Political Practices Commission
P. O. Box 807
Sacramento, California    95804

Re:    FPPC File No. A-90-309

Dear Ms. Stecher:

     This letter confirms our telephone conversation on May 16, 1990, and requests written advice from the Commission pursuant to Government Code §83114(b).

     I am requesting this advice on behalf of my clients: Brad Gates, the incumbent Sheriff of Orange County and a candidate for re-election to that office; Friends of Brad Gates, his controlled campaign committee (the "Committee"); and David R. White II, CPA, the Committee's treasurer.  I am authorized to make this request on behalf of these clients.

     On April 20, 1990, I spoke with you by telephone concerning the application of §85805(a) to the Committee's lease of its office space.  The relevant facts are as follows:

     The Committee leases office space for its use, where it keeps its records from past campaigns, its computer used for communications with supporters, a telephone and answering machine, and other campaign-related material, and its furniture, equipment and supplies, used in Gates's campaigns and in his incidental office holder activities.  The Committee has a written agreement for the lease of this office, for a term of one calendar year, at the rate of $221.76 per month, for a total of $2,661.12 for the one year term.

     The office is leased from OTRA, INC., a California corporation in good standing.  (The lease agreement is written between the Committee and Ortega Equestrian Center, which is a fictitious business name of OTRA, INC., duly filed with the Orange County Clerk.)  OTRA, INC. is the master lessee of the building in which the office is located, and the surrounding

Jill Stecher, Esq.
May 17, 1990
Page 2


property.  Title to the property is in the name of the lessor to
OTRA, INC.  Neither the candidate, nor any member of his
immediate family, nor the Committee's treasurer, has any interest
in title to the property, or in any leasehold interest in the
property except as disclosed in this letter.

All of the shares of stock of OTRA, INC. are held in the
name of Diana L. Gates, Brad Gates's wife.  Brad Gates himself,
of course, has a community property interest in that stock.

The amount of the rent is computed on the basis of a rate of
$1.26 per square foot, per month.  This rate was established by
OTRA, INC. as the fair market value of the office space, based on
rental rates for other office space in the area.

$1,000 of the annual rent is forgiven by OTRA, INC., as a
non-monetary contribution to the Committee, which reports it as
such.  The Committee pays OTRA, INC. the $1,661.12 balance of the
rent, for the lease of the office space.

In summary, the Committee leases office space from a
corporation which is wholly owned by the wife of the candidate
who controls the Committee.

In this factual circumstance, I asked you if, in the opinion
of the Commission, §85805(a) prohibits the Committee from using
campaign funds to pay for the lease of this office space.  You
responded to me by telephone on May 16, and advised me that it is
the opinion of the Commission that, for purposes of §85805(a), a
corporation is considered a legal entity separate from its
shareholders, and that the Committee's use of campaign funds to
pay for lease of this office space is therefore not prohibited by
§85805(a).  I am requesting that you confirm this advice to me in
writing, pursuant to §83114(b).  If you need any further
information before responding, please let me know.

Thank you very much for your assistance in this matter.

Sincerely,

DARRYL R. WOLD

DRW/km

JSTECHER.LTR

DARRYL R. WOLD
ATTORNEY AT LAW
315 AIRWAY AVENUE, SUITE M-I
COSTA MESA, CALIFORNIA 92626
(714) 641-4948    FAX (714) 546-1003

FPPC
APR 30 '90 21 AUG '90

April 25, 1990

Jill Stecher, Esq.
Fair Political Practices Commission
P.O. Box 807
Sacramento, CA  95804

VIA FACSIMILE
TO (916) 327-2026

Re:  Interpretation of §85805 Discussed on April 20

Dear Ms. Stecher:

On Friday, we discussed the interpretation of Section
85805(a) of the Political Reform Act, and whether the terminology
used in Chapter 7 of the Act, concerning conflicts of interest,
might apply to the interpretation of §85805(a).  You indicated
that you would need to analyze this question further before
responding.  I am sending you this letter to explain why I do not
believe that the "conflict of interest" terminology was intended
by the Legislature to apply to §85805(a), and why it does not.

Section 85805(a) prohibits reimbursement or payment of rent
where the lessee is, or legal title to the property resides in, a
candidate, an elected officer, or a member of that person's
immediate family, among others.  The terminology appears to be
carefully chosen so that the prohibition of that section will
apply only to cases literally covered by it - that is, to
prohibit reimbursement for rent paid only where the lessee is one
of the specified persons, and to prohibit payment of rent where
legal title resides in one of the specified persons.

Significantly, §85805 does not use the terminology of
Chapter 7, which concerns a financial "interest" of an individual
(§87100).  The financial "interest" concerning real property is
defined in §87103(b) as a "direct or indirect interest" worth
$1,000 or more, and in §82033 as including real property owned
"directly, indirectly, or beneficially," and including real
property "of any business entity" in which the individual owns,
directly, indirectly, or beneficially, a 10% interest or greater.

Jill Stecher, Esq.
April 25, 1990
Page Two


If the Legislature had wanted §85805(a) to have the same
broad scope as Chapter 7, the Legislature could have prohibited
reimbursement or payment of rent for real property in which the
candidate or other person specified has an interest. The
definition of §82033 would then have been applicable to define
the type of ownership that would be included in §85805(a).

The Legislature was presumably aware of the broad definition
of "interest" in real property in §82033, however, when it
adopted §85805, but carefully used a different terminology. This
different terminology has a narrower scope than does the
definition of "interest" in §82033. As adopted by the
Legislature, §85805(a) prohibits reimbursement or payment of rent
only where the lessee is" or "legal title resides . . . in" one
of the persons specified. (Emphasis added).

This terminology is precise; its meaning is clear and does
not need further definition for clarity; the definition in §82033
of "interest" in real property was presumably not intended by the
Legislature to be applicable because different terminology is
used in §85805; and §20333 is not applicable because §85805 does
not use the term "interest" that is defined in §82033. For these
reasons, the coverage of §85805(a) should be limited to the cases
that fall within its literal language.

We also discussed the interpretation of §85805, in light of
§85301, which limits the amount of campaign contributions except
from a candidate's own funds. If §85805(a) were to be
interpreted to prohibit payment of rent for property in which the
candidate has only an "interest" as defined in §82033, where the
candidate does not have the "legal title" literally required by
§85805(a), the following anomalous results would occur where the
candidate's campaign committee needs space with a fair market
value of more than $1,000 per year:

(1) The campaign committee would be permitted to
have that campaign headquarters in property
leased or owned by the candidate in the
candidate's own name, because the candidate
could make a non-monetary contribution to the
campaign of the full fair market value;

(2) The campaign committee would be permitted to
have the campaign headquarters in property
leased or owned by a corporation unrelated to
the candidate, because the campaign could pay
the full fair market value of the rent to
that corporation; but

Jill Stecher, Esq.
April 25, 1990
Page Three


(3)  The campaign committee would not be permitted
to have the campaign headquarters in property
leased or owned by a corporation in which the
candidate has an ownership interest, because
the corporation could not donate more than
$1,000 of fair market value of rent to the
campaign, under §85301, but the campaign
could not pay the corporation anything, under
that interpretation of §85805(a).

That pattern of results from such an interpretation of
§85805(a) does not have any rational basis or purpose, and it is
not reasonable to think that the Legislature would have intended
it, where the literal language of §85805(a) does not require it.

Ms. Stecher, I appreciate your taking the time to analyze
these legal issues.  Please call me when you are ready to discuss
this further.  If you would like any additional analysis from me,
please let me know.

                                        Sincerely,

                                        [signature]
                                        DARRYL G. WOLD

DRW:ljp

STECHER.LTR

sections.question_synthetic: Whether California Political Reform Act Section 85805(a) prohibits a campaign committee from using campaign funds to pay rent for office space leased from a corporation wholly owned by the candidate's wife, given that the candidate has a community property interest in the corporation's stock.
sections.parsing_notes: No section headers found; LLM: This is an incoming request letter with an attached supplemental memorandum. No formal question/conclusion sections are present in a structured format. The attorney confirms a prior May 16, 1990 telephone conversation with Ms. Stecher where informal advice was given, and requests written confirmation. The April 25 letter provides detailed legislative history argument to support narrow interpretation of Section 85805(a).

embedding.qa_text: QUESTION: Whether California Political Reform Act Section 85805(a) prohibits a campaign committee from using campaign funds to pay rent for office space leased from a corporation wholly owned by the candidate's wife, given that the candidate has a community property interest in the corporation's stock.

embedding.summary: Attorney Darryl R. Wold requests written FPPC advice on behalf of Sheriff Brad Gates and his campaign committee regarding the application of Political Reform Act Section 85805(a) to the committee's lease of office space from a corporation owned by the candidate's wife. The attorney also provides a supplemental legal memorandum arguing that Section 85805(a) should be interpreted narrowly to apply only to literal ownership or legal title, not to indirect interests as defined in Chapter 7 of the Act.
